pages.text:
CHAPTER 11—ACCESSIBILITY 
 
 
SBC 201-CC-2024 
1337 
 
person who needed this facility should not have 
to find someone with a key for the family 
bathroom if the men's and women's bathrooms 
are readily available. This bathroom does count 
towards the required fixture count (see Section 
2902.1.2) and as such must be available. 
1110.2.2  
Water 
closets 
designed 
for 
assisted toileting. Water closets designed for 
assisted toileting shall comply with Sections 
1110.2.2.1 through 1110.2.2.6 . 
❖ 
In Institutional occupancies, such as assisted 
living and nursing homes, many care recipients 
require 
assistance 
in 
toileting 
activities 
(transferring on and off the toilet, support while 
sitting 
down 
or 
standing 
up, 
clothing 
management, etc.). In addition, many care 
recipients living in care settings don't have the 
range of movement or upper-body strength to 
use the standard grab bar arrangement. ICC 
A117.1 requirements can actually make it more 
difficult to assist care recipients as proper 
space is not provided for staff to provide 
assistance from the side. The requirements in 
this section recognize the specific needs of 
caregivers and older adults in care settings and 
provide an equivalent facilitation to ICC 
A117.1. 
 
Dr. Margaret Calkins, in conjunction with the 
Mayer-Rothschild 
Foundation, 
completed 
research on older adults in care settings and 
how best to support them and caregivers in the 
toileting process. This research studied older 
adults toileting independently, as well as those 
needing assistance from one or two caregivers 
and requiring mechanical lift assistance. The 
recommendations from this research were used 
to develop this set of alternative criteria to help 
older adults use the toilet in a safer and more 
supportive manner. 
 
An example of the requirements for assisted 
toileting is provided in Commentary Figure 
1110.2.2. 
1110.2.2.1  
Location. The centerline of the 
water closet shall be not less than 600 mm and not 
greater than 650 mm from one side of the required 
clearance. 
❖ 
ICC A117.1 requires toilets to be located with 
the centerline of the water closet to be placed 
450 mm from the side wall. It also requires a 
wall next to the water closet for mounting a 
grab bar. Often, a care recipient will need 
assistance from two caregivers (one on each 
side) or need assistance on one side of their 
body, but that side happens to be against the 
wall and that space is not wide enough for a 
staff person to get themselves into. This can 
cause staff injuries or lead to care recipient falls 
when a care recipient is not properly 
supported. By pulling the water closet further 
away from the wall, it allows space for a 
caregiver to come alongside the toilet and lift 
safely. 
 
This section does not require a wall alongside 
the water closet at all. It states that the 
centerline of the water closet must be 600 mm 
to 650 mm from one side of the required 
clearance. This means that a water closet could 
be in the middle of a room, if so desired. It 
simply denotes where the measurement of the 
clearance begins. This would also mean that a 
wall or other obstacle cannot be located closer 
than 600 mm from the centerline of the water 
closet on one side. 
1110.2.2.2  
Clearance. Clearance around the 
water 
closet 
shall 
comply 
with 
Sections 
1110.2.2.2.1 through 1110.2.2.2.3 . 
❖ 
Clearance around the water closet is important 
to allow a person with a mobility device to 
maneuver and for staff assistance. 
1110.2.2.2.1  
Clearance 
width. 
Clearance 
around a water closet shall be not less than 1650 
mm in width, measured perpendicularly from the 
side of the clearance that is not less than 600 mm 
and not greater than 650 mm from the water closet 
centerline. 
❖ 
As noted in the commentary for Section 
1110.2.2.1 , space is needed on both sides of 
the water closet to allow for staff to be on either 
or both sides to assist the care recipient in 
standing, sitting or transferring. The 1650 mm 
clearance is offset from the centerline of the 
water closet to provide an “open side” and a 
“narrow side.” The wide side still allows enough 
clear space for a wheelchair user to pull up 
alongside the water closet and execute a side 
slide transfer if they are able to do so. 
1110.2.2.2.2  
Clearance 
depth. 
Clearance 
around the water closet shall be not less than 1950 
mm in depth, measured perpendicularly from the 
rear wall. 
❖ 
The added depth in front of the water closet 
allows space for a mobility device such as a 
wheelchair or walker to be pulled in front of the 
water closet, or to allow space for lift access. 
 
Many care recipients who use wheelchairs can 
still bear weight on one or both legs for short 
periods of time. Many transfer to the water 
closet by pulling up to the front, standing up, 
turning around and then sitting down. More 
space in front of the water closet allows this to 
occur. 
-

1110.2.2.1 through 1110.2.2.6 .

-

pages.text:
CHAPTER 11—ACCESSIBILITY 
 
 
SBC 201-CC-2024 
1338 
 
1110.2.2.2.3  
Clearance overlap. The required 
clearance around the water closet shall permit 
overlaps per ICC A117.1, Section 604.3.3 
❖ 
The water closet clearance can overlap 
elements of the accessible route (path of travel, 
door clearance), including the turning space 
required in the toilet room. 
 
Items that can overlap the clear floor space of 
the water closet without blocking access to the 
water closet include the swing-up grab bars. 
Items that cannot overlap the clear floor space 
for the water closet include counters or the 
accessible lavatory. The spaces at the lavatory 
where the person who uses a wheelchair would 
sit to use those fixtures may overlap the clear 
floor space at the water closet. The required 
clear floor space around the water closet is 
permitted to overlap the clearances in front or 
the floor area of the roll-in shower provided that 
floor slopes do not exceed 1:48 in the area for 
the required clearance. 
1110.2.2.3  
Height. The height of the water 
closet seats shall comply with ICC A117.1, Section 
604.4. 
❖ 
The height of the seat of the water closet in ICC 
A117.1 is 425 mm to 475 mm. A standard water 
closet seat height is 350 mm to 450 mm. A 
higher seat height helps persons with difficulty 
standing and sitting. 
1110.2.2.4  
Swing-up grab bars. Swing-up 
grab bars shall comply with ICC A117.1, Sections 
609.2 and 609.8. Swing-up grab bars shall be 
provided on both sides of the water closet and shall 
comply with all of the following: 
1 
The centerline of the grab bar shall be not less 
than 350 mm and not greater than 400 mm 
from the centerline of the water closet. 
2 
The length of the grab bar is not less than 900 
mm in length, measured from the rear wall to 
the end of the grab bar. 
3 
The top of the grab bar in the down position 
is not less than 750 mm and not greater than 
850 mm above the floor. 
❖ 
The Mayer-Rothschild research demonstrated 
that swing-up grab bars are most usable for the 
majority of older adults. Having bars on both 
sides of the toilet is useful for leverage and 
balance when sitting and standing, as well as 
supporting turning in place when standing to 
transfer from a wheelchair or walker. The 
length of the grab bar must project past the 
front of the toilet for maximum usability. 
 
The ability to swing the bars up and out of the 
way is important for caregiver assistance on 
one or both sides or for lift access. 
 
Where possible, the height of the user should 
be considered when locating the height of the 
swing-up bars and adjusted as needed. Shorter 
users do better with the bars in the lower end of 
the height range. 
1110.2.2.5  
Flush controls. Flush controls 
shall comply with ICC A117.1, Section 604.6. 
❖ 
Flush controls must be accessible from the 
“open side” of the clearance at the water closet. 
The controls can be hand operated or 
automatic. Hand-operated controls must meet 
the standard operable parts requirements for 
graspability and maximum force. 
1110.2.2.6  
Dispensers. 
Toilet 
paper 
dispensers shall be mounted on at least one of the 
swing-up grab bars and the outlet of the dispenser 
shall be located not less than 600 mm and not 
greater than 900 mm from the rear wall. 
❖ 
Locating the toilet paper dispenser on the 
swing-up grab bar ensures that it will be within 
reach of the user. Most grab bar manufacturers 
make clip-on or built-in dispensers. 
1110.2.3  
Standard roll-in-type shower 
compartment designed for assisted bathing. 
Standard 
roll-in-type 
shower 
compartments 
designed for assisted bathing shall comply with 
Sections 1110.2.3.1 through 1110.2.3.9 . 
❖ 
ICC A117.1 accessible roll-in showers work 
well for most wheelchair users who can transfer 
and shower independently. However, for older 
adults and those in care settings the 
requirements can make use of the showers 
more difficult. In nursing homes and assisted 
living facilities many residents are assisted in 
their bathing activities, to various extents, as 
they may not be able to safely manage this 
completely on their own. In newer care models, 
every sleeping room has a private bathroom 
with shower which allows for greater privacy 
and dignity for the care recipient as well as less 
anxiety about going to an unfamiliar space for 
bathing. The requirements in this section 
recognize the specific needs of caregivers and 
older adults in care settings and provide 
equivalent facilitation to ICC A117.1. 
 
Examples of the requirements for assisted 
bathing is provided in Commentary Figures 
Figure 1110.2.3(1), Figure 1110.2.3(2) and 
Figure 1110.2.3(3). 
1110.2.3.1  
Size. Standard roll-in-type shower 
compartments shall have a clear inside dimension 
-
pages.code_text:
CHAPTER 11—ACCESSIBILITY 
 
 
SBC 201-CC-2024 
1338 
 
1110.2.2.2.3  
Clearance overlap. The required 
clearance around the water closet shall permit 
overlaps per ICC A117.1, Section 604.3.3
pages.commentary:
The water closet clearance can overlap 
elements of the accessible route (path of travel, 
door clearance), including the turning space 
required in the toilet room. 
 
Items that can overlap the clear floor space of 
the water closet without blocking access to the 
water closet include the swing-up grab bars. 
Items that cannot overlap the clear floor space 
for the water closet include counters or the 
accessible lavatory. The spaces at the lavatory 
where the person who uses a wheelchair would 
sit to use those fixtures may overlap the clear 
floor space at the water closet. The required 
clear floor space around the water closet is 
permitted to overlap the clearances in front or 
the floor area of the roll-in shower provided that 
floor slopes do not exceed 1:48 in the area for 
the required clearance. 
1110.2.2.3  
Height. The height of the water 
closet seats shall comply with ICC A117.1, Section 
604.4.

❖ The height of the seat of the water closet in ICC 
A117.1 is 425 mm to 475 mm. A standard water 
closet seat height is 350 mm to 450 mm. A 
higher seat height helps persons with difficulty 
standing and sitting. 
1110.2.2.4  
Swing-up grab bars. Swing-up 
grab bars shall comply with ICC A117.1, Sections 
609.2 and 609.8. Swing-up grab bars shall be 
provided on both sides of the water closet and shall 
comply with all of the following: 
1 
The centerline of the grab bar shall be not less 
than 350 mm and not greater than 400 mm 
from the centerline of the water closet. 
2 
The length of the grab bar is not less than 900 
mm in length, measured from the rear wall to 
the end of the grab bar. 
3 
The top of the grab bar in the down position 
is not less than 750 mm and not greater than 
850 mm above the floor.

❖ The Mayer-Rothschild research demonstrated 
that swing-up grab bars are most usable for the 
majority of older adults. Having bars on both 
sides of the toilet is useful for leverage and 
balance when sitting and standing, as well as 
supporting turning in place when standing to 
transfer from a wheelchair or walker. The 
length of the grab bar must project past the 
front of the toilet for maximum usability. 
 
The ability to swing the bars up and out of the 
way is important for caregiver assistance on 
one or both sides or for lift access. 
 
Where possible, the height of the user should 
be considered when locating the height of the 
swing-up bars and adjusted as needed. Shorter 
users do better with the bars in the lower end of 
the height range. 
1110.2.2.5  
Flush controls. Flush controls 
shall comply with ICC A117.1, Section 604.6.

❖ Flush controls must be accessible from the 
“open side” of the clearance at the water closet. 
The controls can be hand operated or 
automatic. Hand-operated controls must meet 
the standard operable parts requirements for 
graspability and maximum force. 
1110.2.2.6  
Dispensers. 
Toilet 
paper 
dispensers shall be mounted on at least one of the 
swing-up grab bars and the outlet of the dispenser 
shall be located not less than 600 mm and not 
greater than 900 mm from the rear wall.

❖ Locating the toilet paper dispenser on the 
swing-up grab bar ensures that it will be within 
reach of the user. Most grab bar manufacturers 
make clip-on or built-in dispensers. 
1110.2.3  
Standard roll-in-type shower 
compartment designed for assisted bathing. 
Standard 
roll-in-type 
shower 
compartments 
designed for assisted bathing shall comply with 
Sections 1110.2.3.1 through 1110.2.3.9 .

❖ ICC A117.1 accessible roll-in showers work 
well for most wheelchair users who can transfer 
and shower independently. However, for older 
adults and those in care settings the 
requirements can make use of the showers 
more difficult. In nursing homes and assisted 
living facilities many residents are assisted in 
their bathing activities, to various extents, as 
they may not be able to safely manage this 
completely on their own. In newer care models, 
every sleeping room has a private bathroom 
with shower which allows for greater privacy 
and dignity for the care recipient as well as less 
anxiety about going to an unfamiliar space for 
bathing. The requirements in this section 
recognize the specific needs of caregivers and 
older adults in care settings and provide 
equivalent facilitation to ICC A117.1. 
 
Examples of the requirements for assisted 
bathing is provided in Commentary Figures 
Figure 1110.2.3(1), Figure 1110.2.3(2) and 
Figure 1110.2.3(3). 
1110.2.3.1  
Size. Standard roll-in-type shower 
compartments shall have a clear inside dimension 
-

pages.text:
CHAPTER 11—ACCESSIBILITY 
 
 
SBC 201-CC-2024 
1339 
 
of not less than 1500 mm in width and 750 mm in 
depth, measured at the center point of opposing 
sides. An entry not less than 1500 mm in width shall 
be provided. 
❖ 
This section recognizes the minimum size 
shower area that is required for an accessible 
roll-in shower. The 1500 mm by 750 mm 
minimum dimensions are consistent with ICC 
A117.1 for accessible roll-in showers. 
 
Sometimes, larger roll-in showers and more 
open shower configurations work better for 
older adults and caregivers. Larger showers 
also allow for bathing of care recipients who 
must be bathed in a semi- or fully reclined 
position. These care recipients simply cannot fit 
in a standard shower stall. It is not the intent of 
these provisions to require three walls, but 
instead to have a minimum size for the shower 
base. Some shower designs use only two walls, 
or the entire bathroom is designed as a 
“European shower”—a wet room with the 
shower head located in the corner of the room. 
1110.2.3.2  
Clearance. A clearance of not less 
than 1500 mm in length adjacent to the 1500 mm 
width of the open face of the shower compartment, 
and not less than 750 mm in depth, shall be 
provided. 
Exceptions: 
1 
A lavatory complying with ICC A117.1, 
Section 606 shall be permitted at one end of 
the clearance. 
2 
Where the shower compartment exceeds 
minimum sizes, the clear floor space shall be 
placed adjacent to the grab bars and not less 
than 750 mm from the back wall. 
❖ 
This 
section 
recognizes 
the 
minimum 
clearances needed to access this shower. A 
clear space of 750 mm by 1500 mm must be 
provided to access the shower to allow for a 
possible transfer for the care recipients to a 
bathing seat in the shower. Per Exception 1, a 
lavatory with knee clearance is permitted to 
encroach into this clearance per ICC A117.1 
Section 
606 
[see 
Commentary 
Figure 
1110.2.3(1)]. The clear floor space on the 
“open side” of the water closet, as described in 
the commentary to Section 1110.2.2.2.1 , may 
double as this shower access space, or if the 
water closet is located immediately adjacent to 
the shower area, the shower access space 
would be provided in front of the water closet 
[see Commentary Figure 1110.2.3(2) and 
Figure 1110.2.3(3)]. 
1110.2.3.3  
Grab bars. Grab bars shall comply 
with ICC A117.1, Section 609 and shall be provided 
in accordance with Sections 1110.2.3.3.1 and 
1110.2.3.3.2 . In standard roll-in-type shower 
compartments, grab bars shall be provided on three 
walls. Where multiple grab bars are used, required 
horizontal grab bars shall be installed at the same 
height above the floor. Grab bars can be separate 
bars or one continuous bar. 
❖ 
The ICC A117.1 reference to Section 609 is for 
the cross section, spacing, height, surface 
hazards, fittings, installation and structural 
strength for grab bars. The configuration with 
three walls is consistent with a minimum size 
accessible shower. 
1110.2.3.3.1  
Back-wall grab bar. The back-
wall grab bar shall extend the length of the back 
wall and extend within 150 mm maximum from the 
two adjacent sidewalls. 
Exception: The back-wall grab bar shall not be 
required to exceed 1200 mm in length. The rear 
grab bar shall be located with one end within 150 
mm maximum of a sidewall with a grab bar 
complying with Section 1110.2.3.3.2 . 
❖ 
The base requirement has the back-wall grab 
bar extending the length of the back wall. The 
exception recognizes that where a shower area 
is longer than 1500 mm it is not necessary to 
extend the back-wall grab bar for the entire 
length of the wall. The back-wall and sidewall 
grab bars must be located in the area of the 
showerhead or hand shower. 
1110.2.3.3.2  
Sidewall grab bars. The sidewall 
grab bars shall extend the length of the wall and 
extend within 150 mm of the adjacent back wall. 
Exceptions: 
1 
The sidewall grab bar shall not be required to 
exceed 750 mm in length. The side grab bar 
shall be located with one end within 150 mm 
of the back wall with a grab bar complying 
with Section 1110.2.3.3.1 . 
2 
Where the sidewalls are located 1800 mm or 
greater apart, a grab bar is not required on one 
of the sidewalls. 
❖ 
The base requirement has the sidewall grab 
bar extending the length of the side wall. 
Exception 1 establishes a grab bar length that 
need not be exceeded where showers exceed 
minimum size. Exception 2 recognizes that if a 
shower area is configured where the side walls 
are greater than 1800 mm apart, a sidewall 
grab bar on the wall furthest from the shower 
-
pages.code_text:
CHAPTER 11—ACCESSIBILITY 
 
 
SBC 201-CC-2024 
1339 
 
of not less than 1500 mm in width and 750 mm in 
depth, measured at the center point of opposing 
sides. An entry not less than 1500 mm in width shall 
be provided.

1110.2.3.3.2 . In standard roll-in-type shower

walls. Where multiple grab bars are used, required

bars or one continuous bar.

❖ The ICC A117.1 reference to Section 609 is for 
the cross section, spacing, height, surface 
hazards, fittings, installation and structural 
strength for grab bars. The configuration with 
three walls is consistent with a minimum size 
accessible shower. 
1110.2.3.3.1  
Back-wall grab bar. The back-
wall grab bar shall extend the length of the back 
wall and extend within 150 mm maximum from the 
two adjacent sidewalls. 
Exception: The back-wall grab bar shall not be 
required to exceed 1200 mm in length. The rear 
grab bar shall be located with one end within 150 
mm maximum of a sidewall with a grab bar 
complying with Section 1110.2.3.3.2 .

❖ The base requirement has the back-wall grab 
bar extending the length of the back wall. The

is longer than 1500 mm it is not necessary to 
extend the back-wall grab bar for the entire 
length of the wall. The back-wall and sidewall 
grab bars must be located in the area of the

1110.2.3.3.2  
Sidewall grab bars. The sidewall 
grab bars shall extend the length of the wall and 
extend within 150 mm of the adjacent back wall. 
Exceptions: 
1 
The sidewall grab bar shall not be required to 
exceed 750 mm in length. The side grab bar 
shall be located with one end within 150 mm 
of the back wall with a grab bar complying 
with Section 1110.2.3.3.1 . 
2 
Where the sidewalls are located 1800 mm or 
greater apart, a grab bar is not required on one 
of the sidewalls.

❖ The base requirement has the sidewall grab 
bar extending the length of the side wall. 
Exception 1 establishes a grab bar length that 
need not be exceeded where showers exceed 
minimum size. Exception 2 recognizes that if a 
shower area is configured where the side walls 
are greater than 1800 mm apart, a sidewall 
grab bar on the wall furthest from the shower 
-

pages.text:
CHAPTER 11—ACCESSIBILITY 
 
 
SBC 201-CC-2024 
1340 
 
head or hand shower may not be useful as it is 
too far away from the shower user and not 
needed for transfer in and out of the 
compartment. 
1110.2.3.4  
Seats. Wall-mounted folding seats 
shall not be installed. 
❖ 
Many older adults have difficulty transferring to 
and from a built-in shower bench due to limited 
mobility and upper arm strength. The use of a 
mobile shower bench or chair that has grab 
bars or arms attached for added assistance 
and security is preferred. Many care recipients 
are fearful of falls and wall mounted seats do 
not always feel very secure to them, which 
makes them not willing to use them. Further, if 
a caregiver is assisting the care recipient in 
bathing, the fixed location of the seat makes it 
very difficult to reach and clean all parts of the 
recipient. A mobile chair or bench allows the 
care recipient to be repositioned as needed. 
 
Where fixed seats are installed in showers but 
not used, they take up space and are often left 
in the up position, which can expose sharper 
parts of the mechanism and cause injury to care 
recipients or staff. 
1110.2.3.5  
Controls and hand showers. In 
standard roll-in-type showers, the controls and hand 
shower shall be located not less than 950 mm and 
not greater than 1200 mm above the shower floor. 
Controls shall be located to facilitate caregiver 
access. 
❖ 
When caregivers are assisting residents with 
bathing, the ICC A117.1 requirements for 
location of controls makes it very difficult to 
adjust water temperature and turn water flow 
on and off without the caregiver getting wet. By 
allowing flexibility in where the shower controls 
are located, this allows for better placement for 
use by both independent users who may use a 
mobile chair, or for caregivers. When building 
user input is not possible during design, a 
suggested location for the controls and hand 
shower would be on the long, or back wall of 
the roll-in shower, located 750 mm minimum 
from one of the side walls. A means of shutting 
off the water in the showerhead, or diverting 
water flow, without moving the temperature 
control handle should be provided. 
 
In addition, attention should be paid to the 
direction of the water spray to minimize water 
spray outside the shower area. If a stationary 
shower head is provided in addition to the hand 
shower, it should be located on the side wall to 
minimize water outside the shower area. A 
shower 
curtain 
can 
assist 
with 
water 
containment but is not required. When 
caregivers are assisting with bathing, a curtain 
would most likely be open to allow access to the 
care recipient. 
1110.2.3.6  
Hand showers. Hand showers 
shall comply with ICC A117.1, Section 608.5. 
❖ 
A means of shutting off the water in the 
showerhead, or diverting water flow, without 
moving the temperature control handle should 
be provided. This helps a caregiver wash the 
care recipient without water flowing, and then 
turn the water flow back on with some 
assurance the temperature will be at the 
desired level, minimizing shocking the care 
recipient with too cold or too hot water. This is 
consistent with SBC 701 Section 412.3. 
1110.2.3.7  
Thresholds. 
Thresholds 
shall 
comply with ICC A117.1, Section 608.6. 
❖ 
Roll-in shower thresholds should be minimized 
as much as possible to allow for mobile shower 
benches and chairs to be moved in and out of 
the shower easily. A threshold with a 12.5 mm 
maximum height will help reduce the amount of 
water that will reach the bathroom floor while 
still allowing the front wheels of wheelchairs or 
bathing chair to easily move into the shower 
stall. 
1110.2.3.8  
Shower 
enclosures. 
Shower 
compartment enclosures for shower compartments 
shall comply with ICC A117.1, Section 608.7. 
❖ 
Enclosures must be mounted in a manner that 
allows the user full accessibility without 
defeating any of the design features required 
by ICC A117.1. Curtains on a standard rod 
would typically not block a transfer; however, 
shower doors would most likely be an 
obstruction. Tracks on the shower threshold 
may prevent access into the shower stall. Even 
in a transfer situation, the wheelchair user may 
need to move the small front wheels of the 
chair into the stall to make the transfer. 
1110.2.3.9  
Water 
temperature. 
Water 
temperature shall comply with ICC A117.1, Section 
608.8. 
❖ 
Showers shall deliver water that is 50°C 
maximum. The temperature limitation for the 
water is to prevent accidental scalding. Options 
for controlling the temperature of the water at 
the 
shower 
are 
a 
balanced 
pressure, 
thermostatic 
or 
combination 
balanced 
pressure/thermostatic valve. This is consistent 
with SBC 701 Section 412.3. 
1110.2.4  
Water 
closet 
compartment. 
Where water closet compartments are provided in a 
toilet room or bathing room, at least 5 percent of the 
-

head or hand shower may not be useful as it is 
too far away from the shower user and not 
needed for transfer in and out of the 
compartment.

Seats. Wall-mounted folding seats 
shall not be installed.

shall

shall comply with ICC A117.1, Section 608.7.

❖ Enclosures must be mounted in a manner that 
allows the user full accessibility without 
defeating any of the design features required 
by ICC A117.1. Curtains on a standard rod 
would typically not block a transfer; however, 
shower doors would most likely be an

may prevent access into the shower stall. Even 
in a transfer situation, the wheelchair user may 
need to move the small front wheels of the 
chair into the stall to make the transfer. 
1110.2.3.9  
Water 
temperature. 
Water 
temperature shall comply with ICC A117.1, Section 
608.8.

❖ Showers shall deliver water that is 50°C 
maximum. The temperature limitation for the 
water is to prevent accidental scalding. Options 
for controlling the temperature of the water at 
the 
shower 
are 
a 
balanced 
pressure, 
thermostatic 
or 
combination 
balanced 
pressure/thermostatic valve. This is consistent 
with SBC 701 Section 412.3. 
1110.2.4  
Water 
closet 
compartment. 
Where water closet compartments are provided in a 
toilet room or bathing room, at least 5 percent of the 
-

pages.text:
CHAPTER 11—ACCESSIBILITY 
 
 
SBC 201-CC-2024 
1341 
 
total number of compartments shall be wheelchair 
accessible. Where the combined total water closet 
compartments and urinals provided in a toilet room 
or bathing room is six or more, at least 5 percent of 
the total number of compartments shall be 
ambulatory accessible, provided in addition to the 
wheelchair-accessible compartment. 
❖ 
There are different configurations of water 
closet compartments that facilitate different 
degrees of physical disability. The provisions of 
ICC A117.1 establish the configuration and 
dimensional requirements for various types of 
water closet compartments. A wheelchair-
accessible compartment is one in which 
sufficient space is provided for the wheelchair 
to 
enter 
completely 
the 
water 
closet 
compartment 
[see 
Commentary 
Figures 
1110.2.4(2) and 1110.2.4(3)]. The wheelchair 
user then transfers from the wheelchair to the 
water closet in order to utilize the fixture. It is 
important that the required clear floor space be 
maintained. The layouts for compartments did 
not intend for a lavatory to be provided within 
the minimum-sized compartment. If a lavatory 
is located within a compartment, it must meet 
the same provisions as a single-occupant toilet 
room.  
 
An ambulatory-accessible compartment is 
intended to facilitate use by a person with a 
mobility impairment that necessitates the use of 
a walking aid, such as a cane or walker [see 
Commentary 
Figure 
1110.2.4(1)]. 
An 
ambulatory-accessible 
water 
closet 
compartment is not intended to be utilized by a 
person in a wheelchair. The 900 mm width is 
intended to allow standing persons to support 
themselves utilizing the grab bars on both 
sides. A wider compartment would not allow 
adequate bearing. Since these provisions are 
intended to address, within reason, the needs 
of both ranges of mobility impairment, this 
section 
requires 
a 
wheelchair-accessible 
compartment in all cases. In larger toilet rooms 
(i.e., those with a total of six or more water 
closet 
compartments 
and 
urinals), 
one 
ambulatory-accessible 
compartment 
is 
required 
in 
addition 
to 
the 
wheelchair-
accessible compartment. In very large facilities 
with more than 20 toilet compartments in a 
single toilet room, an additional accessible and 
ambulatory water closet may be required to 
meet the 5-percent requirements.  
 
This section is not intended to increase the 
required number of fixtures beyond that 
required by SBC 701. For example, if a toilet 
room contains 10 water closets, eight water 
closet compartments may be of conventional 
design, one water closet compartment must be 
wheelchair accessible and one must be 
ambulatory accessible. 
1110.2.5  
Lavatories. Where lavatories are 
provided, at least 5 percent, but not less than one, 
shall be accessible. Where an accessible lavatory is 
located within the accessible water closet 
compartment at least one additional accessible 
lavatory shall be provided in the multi-compartment 
toilet room outside the water closet compartment. 
Where the total lavatories provided in a toilet room 
or bathing facility is six or more, at least one 
lavatory with enhanced reach ranges shall be 
provided. 
❖ 
A lavatory is a type of sink for which the primary 
purpose is hand washing. This section 
provides scoping requirements for lavatories 
located in spaces not specifically addressed or 
exempted by Section 1110.2 . If a lavatory is 
provided in toilet and bathing rooms specifically 
exempted by Section 1110.2 , it is not required 
to be accessible. Lavatories that are part of an 
employee workstation are exempted under 
Section 1103.2.2 . An example of a lavatory 
that was part of an employee’s work area would 
be the lavatories found in the exam rooms. The 
lavatories are there for the nurses and doctors 
to wash their hands before examining a patient. 
 
Where lavatories are provided in other areas, a 
minimum of 5 percent, but not less than one, is 
required to be accessible via a front approach 
with appropriate knee and toe clearances.  
 
A designer can choose to locate an accessible 
lavatory actually within the accessible toilet 
stall for customer convenience. If this is the 
case, that cannot be the only accessible 
lavatory within the toilet room. An additional 
accessible lavatory must be provided outside 
of the accessible stall. In large facilities, where 
the designer has located six or more lavatories 
within one toilet facility (similar to the 
ambulatory stall), one of the lavatories must 
also be provided with enhanced reach range 
for the faucet controls in accordance with 
Section 606.5 of ICC A117.1. This would 
require the faucets to be located on the side of 
the lavatory or provide access to the side of 
one of the lavatories, or to have automatic 
controls. This is to address the needs of 
persons who may have a limited reach over the 
lavatory where they may have difficulty 
reaching the controls. The lavatory with 
enhanced reach range could be the same 
lavatory as the accessible lavatory, or it could 
be another lavatory.  
 
There are allowances in ICC A117.1 for 
lavatories specifically designed for children’s 
-
pages.code_text:
CHAPTER 11—ACCESSIBILITY 
 
 
SBC 201-CC-2024 
1341 
 
total number of compartments shall be wheelchair 
accessible. Where the combined total water closet 
compartments and urinals provided in a toilet room 
or bathing room is six or more, at least 5 percent of 
the total number of compartments shall be 
ambulatory accessible, provided in addition to the 
wheelchair-accessible compartment.
pages.commentary:
There are different configurations of water 
closet compartments that facilitate different 
degrees of physical disability. The provisions of 
ICC A117.1 establish the configuration and 
dimensional requirements for various types of 
water closet compartments. A wheelchair-
accessible compartment is one in which 
sufficient space is provided for the wheelchair 
to 
enter 
completely 
the 
water 
closet 
compartment 
[see 
Commentary 
Figures 
1110.2.4(2) and 1110.2.4(3)]. The wheelchair 
user then transfers from the wheelchair to the 
water closet in order to utilize the fixture. It is 
important that the required clear floor space be 
maintained. The layouts for compartments did 
not intend for a lavatory to be provided within 
the minimum-sized compartment. If a lavatory 
is located within a compartment, it must meet 
the same provisions as a single-occupant toilet 
room.  
 
An ambulatory-accessible compartment is 
intended to facilitate use by a person with a 
mobility impairment that necessitates the use of 
a walking aid, such as a cane or walker [see 
Commentary 
Figure 
1110.2.4(1)]. 
An 
ambulatory-accessible 
water 
closet 
compartment is not intended to be utilized by a 
person in a wheelchair. The 900 mm width is 
intended to allow standing persons to support 
themselves utilizing the grab bars on both 
sides. A wider compartment would not allow 
adequate bearing. Since these provisions are 
intended to address, within reason, the needs 
of both ranges of mobility impairment, this 
section 
requires 
a 
wheelchair-accessible 
compartment in all cases. In larger toilet rooms 
(i.e., those with a total of six or more water 
closet 
compartments 
and 
urinals), 
one 
ambulatory-accessible 
compartment 
is 
required 
in 
addition 
to 
the 
wheelchair-
accessible compartment. In very large facilities 
with more than 20 toilet compartments in a 
single toilet room, an additional accessible and 
ambulatory water closet may be required to 
meet the 5-percent requirements.

required number of fixtures beyond that 
required by SBC 701. For example, if a toilet 
room contains 10 water closets, eight water 
closet compartments may be of conventional 
design, one water closet compartment must be 
wheelchair accessible and one must be 
ambulatory accessible.

compartment at least one additional accessible

toilet room outside the water closet compartment. 
Where the total lavatories provided in a toilet room 
or bathing facility is six or more, at least one 
lavatory with enhanced reach ranges shall be 
provided.

Section 606.5 of ICC A117.1. This would

controls. This is to address the needs of

pages.text:
CHAPTER 11—ACCESSIBILITY 
 
 
SBC 201-CC-2024 
1342 
 
use. This may be appropriate in areas such as 
day care facilities and certain areas of 
elementary schools. 
1110.3  Sinks. Where sinks are provided, at least 5 
percent but not less than one provided in accessible 
spaces shall be accessible. 
Exception: Mop or service sinks are not required to 
be accessible. 
❖ 
A sink may have numerous functions, unlike a 
lavatory, which is primarily provided for hand 
washing. Sinks in kitchens, kitchenettes and 
classrooms are examples that may have 
scoping 
and 
accessibility 
requirements 
different than those for lavatories. Where sinks 
are provided, a minimum of 5 percent, but not 
fewer than one, is required to be accessible via 
a front approach with knee and toe clearances. 
There is a specific exception here for mop or 
service-type sinks. Service sinks include a 
wide variety of specialized sinks manufactured 
for multiple or specific functions. Service sinks 
are typically sized to be very deep and 
positioned for their function, which often 
renders them incapable of being accessible. 
Also, where a sink is part of an individual 
workstation, that sink is not required to be 
accessible 
in 
accordance 
with 
Section 
1103.2.2. 
 
A forward approach to sinks is not always 
required (see commentary, Section 1110.4). 
 
There are allowances in ICC A117.1 for sinks 
specifically designed for children's use. This 
may be appropriate in areas such as day care 
facilities and certain areas of elementary 
schools. 
1110.4  Kitchens 
and 
kitchenettes. 
Where 
kitchens and kitchenettes are provided in accessible 
spaces or rooms, they shall be accessible. 
 
Kitchens, kitchenettes and wet bars in spaces 
such as community rooms or break rooms must 
be accessible. Kitchens, kitchenettes and wet 
bars within Accessible, Type A and Type B 
units shall comply with ICC A117.1, Chapter 10 
(see exception, Section 1110.1). An accessible 
kitchen would include clear floor spaces at all 
appliances, a sink with a front approach and a 
work surface with a front approach (see Section 
804 of ICC A117.1 for specific details). 
Kitchenettes (where a cooktop or conventional 
range is not provided) allow a side approach to 
the sink (Section 606 of ICC A117.1) and do not 
require an accessible work surface. A common 
mistake is to forget that the sink must be set at 
a maximum height of 850 mm to allow for reach 
to the faucets (see Commentary Figure 
1110.4). 
 
Commercial kitchens are considered employee 
work areas. See the commentaries to Sections 
1103.2.2 and 1104.3.1. Kitchenettes that are 
part of an employee break room are specifically 
excluded in the definition of “Employee work 
area” (see Chapter 2) and must be accessible. 
1110.5  Drinking fountains. Where drinking 
fountains are provided on an exterior site, on a floor 
or within a secured area, the drinking fountains 
shall be provided in accordance with Sections 
1110.5.1 and 1110.5.2 .  
❖ 
This 
section 
establishes 
a 
reasonable 
threshold for the required number of accessible 
drinking fountains. It should be noted that this 
section does not require the installation of 
drinking fountains where none are required or 
provided. Chapter 29 (which is duplicated from 
Section 403 of SBC 701) contains criteria 
indicating the number of drinking fountains that 
are required based on occupancy. There are 
spaces that are not required to have drinking 
fountains. Examples are spaces with fewer 
than 15 occupants and spaces that serve 
water, such as a restaurant. 
 
Current requirements for plumbing fixtures 
(see Table 2902.1 of this code and Table 403 
of SBC 701) stipulate only one drinking 
fountain for many facilities or tenant spaces. 
The provisions in Section 1110.5.1 of this code 
and Section 410.3.1 of SBC 701 stipulate a 
minimum of two drinking fountains for these 
facilities and spaces in order to meet the 
high/low criteria. Additionally, the plumbing 
requirements would allow for 50 percent of the 
drinking fountains to be substituted with water 
dispensers (see Section 410.4 of SBC 701). 
While the provisions in Sections 1110.5.1 of 
this code and 1110.5.2 and Section 410.3.1 of 
SBC 701 would not prohibit the substitution, 
the requirement for two drinking fountains 
would effectively negate this substitution 
unless there were at least three drinking 
fountains required for a floor. SBC 701 has 
defined “Drinking fountain,” “Water cooler” and 
“Water dispenser” in an effort to clarify the 
differences 
between 
drinking 
fountains 
(including water coolers) and stand-alone 
water dispensers. A water dispenser cannot be 
considered as an alternative to either the high 
or low drinking fountain. However, water 
dispensers that are permanently installed 
would have to comply with Section 1110.15 for 
operable parts. 
 
SBC 701 includes provisions for travel distance 
to a drinking fountain (see Section 403.5 of 
SBC 701), which are 152 m and one-story 
maximum travel. In malls, the maximum travel 
is reduced to 91 m. 
-

(including water coolers) and stand-alone

pages.text:
CHAPTER 11—ACCESSIBILITY 
 
 
SBC 201-CC-2024 
1343 
 
1110.5.1  
Minimum number. No fewer than 
two drinking fountains shall be provided. One 
drinking 
fountain 
shall 
comply 
with 
the 
requirements for people who use a wheelchair and 
one drinking fountain shall comply with the 
requirements for standing persons. 
Exceptions: 
1 
A single drinking fountain with two separate 
spouts that complies with the requirements 
for people who use a wheelchair and standing 
persons shall be permitted to be substituted 
for two separate drinking fountains. 
2 
Where drinking fountains are primarily for 
children’s use, drinking fountains for people 
using wheelchairs shall be permitted to 
comply with the children’s provisions in ICC 
A117.1 and drinking fountains for standing 
children shall be permitted to provide the 
spout at 760 mm minimum above the floor. 
❖ 
Where a single drinking fountain is provided, 
this section mandates a minimum of two 
fixtures be provided: one for persons seated in 
a wheelchair (low) and one for standing 
persons (high). The high and low drinking 
fountains that serve a facility need not be 
provided at the same location in the facility. If 
the high and low drinking fountains are 
remotely located, signage is required to 
indicate the location of the other fountains (see 
Section 1112.3 ). Technical criteria for both 
high and low fountains are located in Section 
602 of ICC A117.1 (see Commentary igure 
1110.5.1). The drinking fountains must be 
located so that they are not protruding objects 
(see Section 1003.3). If a water bottle filler is 
provided, 
it 
must 
meet 
operable 
parts 
requirements (see Section 1110.15 ). 
 
Exception 1 allows for a single drinking 
fountain to meet the requirements for both high 
and low heights by providing two spouts at one 
bowl; however, at this time there are only a few 
drinking fountains on the market that can meet 
this criterion. See the commentary to Section 
1110.5 . 
 
Exception 2 allows for both the high and low 
drinking fountains to be designed for children. 
There are technical criteria in A117.1 for 
wheelchair-accessible 
drinking 
fountains 
specifically designed for children who are age 
12 and younger. This may be appropriate for 
facilities such as day care facilities and certain 
areas of elementary schools. 
1110.5.2  
More 
than 
the 
minimum 
number. Where more than the minimum number of 
drinking fountains specified in Section 1110.5.1 is 
provided, 50 percent of the total number of drinking 
fountains provided shall comply with 
the 
requirements for persons who use a wheelchair and 
50 percent of the total number of drinking fountains 
provided shall comply with the requirements for 
standing persons. 
Exceptions: 
1 
Where 50 percent of the drinking fountains 
yields a fraction, 50 percent shall be 
permitted to be rounded up or down, 
provided that the total number of drinking 
fountains complying with this section equals 
100 percent of the drinking fountains. 
2 
Where drinking fountains are primarily for 
children’s use, drinking fountains for people 
using wheelchairs shall be permitted to 
comply with the children’s provisions in ICC 
A117.1 and drinking fountains for standing 
children shall be permitted to provide the 
spout at 760 mm minimum above the floor. 
❖ 
When an even number of drinking fountains is 
provided, half must accommodate persons 
seated in wheelchairs (low) and half must 
accommodate 
standing 
persons 
(high). 
Exception 1 addresses when an odd number of 
drinking fountains is provided. 
 
An example: 
• 
Two drinking fountains are required by 
Section 1110.5.1 and Section 410.2 of SBC 
701. 
• 
Seven drinking fountains are provided. 
• 
Fifty percent of seven is three and one-half. 
• 
Rounding up yields four; rounding down 
yields three. 
• 
Therefore, there are two choices: 
• 
Provide four low and three high fountains: or 
• 
Provide three low and four high fountains. 
• 
Both choices comply since the complying 
fixtures total 100 percent. 
❖ 
This logic applies whenever an odd number of 
three or more drinking fountains is provided, 
regardless of the total quantity. This is based 
on the number provided—not the number 
required. In accordance with Section 410.3 of 
SBC 701, a designer/owner could switch out 
some of the required drinking fountains for 
bottled water dispensers (see commentary to 
Section 1110.5 ).  
 
Exception 2 is consistent with the same 
exception in Section 1110.5.2 , allowing for 
-

pages.text:
CHAPTER 11—ACCESSIBILITY 
 
 
SBC 201-CC-2024 
1344 
 
both high and low drinking fountains to be 
designed for children. There are technical 
criteria in ICC A117.1 for wheelchair accessible 
drinking fountains specifically designed for 
children who are age 12 and younger. This may 
be appropriate for day care facilities and 
certain areas of elementary schools. 
1110.6  1110.6 Bottle-filling stations.  Where 
bottle-filling stations are provided, they shall be 
accessible. 
Exception: Bottle-filling stations over drinking 
fountains for standing persons are not required to be 
accessible, provided that bottle-filling stations are 
also located over the drinking fountains for persons 
using wheelchairs. 
❖ 
In the interest of reducing the use of disposable 
bottles for water, many schools are providing 
bottle-filling 
stations. 
Due 
to 
security 
limitations, bottle-filling stations are also being 
provided in airports. All operable parts intended 
for use by occupants must be accessible. 
 
The exception is in recognition that the 
requirements for standing drinking fountains 
potentially will not allow for compliant reach 
over an obstruction. When the bottle-filling 
stations are provided over the wheelchair 
drinking fountains, having them also over the 
standing drinking fountains would be providing 
redundant facilities. 
1110.7  Saunas 
and 
steam 
rooms. 
Where 
provided, saunas and steam rooms shall be 
accessible. 
Exception: Where saunas or steam rooms are 
clustered at a single location, at least 5 percent of 
the saunas and steam rooms, but not less than one, 
of each type in each cluster shall be accessible. 
❖ 
Since 2009, ICC A117.1 has included technical 
requirements for saunas and steam rooms. 
The scoping in SBC 201 is consistent with 
bathrooms and coordinates with the 2010 ADA 
Standard. What constitutes a “cluster” is 
subjective; however, typically the rooms would 
have to have entrances immediately adjacent 
to or across the hall from one another to be 
considered “clustered.” 
 
The requirements in the standard provide for 
entering the room, closing the door and then 
transferring to the bench in the room. Removing 
the wheelchair from the space is not addressed, 
so perhaps a special chair might be utilized. 
Section 914 of SBC 501 addresses safety 
requirements for sauna rooms. 
1110.8  Elevators. Passenger elevators on an 
accessible route shall be accessible and comply 
with Chapter 30. 
❖ 
This section requires all passenger elevators 
on an accessible route to be accessible. The 
reference to Chapter 30 requires all passenger 
elevators to conform to general elevator safety 
standards as well as accessibility standards. 
This section is not intended to prohibit service 
elevators, Limited Use Limited Access (LULA) 
elevators, private residence elevators or 
platform lifts (as permitted by Section 1110.9). 
Service, LULA and private residence elevators 
are recognized as types of passenger lifts by 
ASME A17.1/CSA B44. 
 
Elevators are defined in ASME A17.1 as “a 
hoisting and lowering mechanism, equipped 
with a car, that moves within guides and serves 
two or more landings … “ The limitations of use 
for the different types of elevators are 
controlled by the standard. 
 
ASME 
limits 
the 
occupants 
in 
“freight 
elevators” to just the operator and the people 
needed to load and unload the freight. Also 
“construction 
elevators” 
are 
limited 
to 
construction purposes only. Only passenger-
type elevators can serve as part of an 
accessible route. Therefore, freight and 
construction types of elevators are not required 
to be accessible. 
 
The term “service elevator” is not defined in 
ASME A17.1. This is a slang term used by 
building owners to separate the “back of the 
house” passenger elevator used by staff (and 
typically designed to accommodate a heavier 
load) from the passenger elevators used by the 
general public for circulation. As part of an 
accessible route to employee work areas, a 
service elevator is required to be accessible. 
 
LULA elevators have a maximum vertical travel 
distance of 7.5 m. Private residence elevators 
are limited to within or serving individual 
dwelling units. 
 
It 
should 
also 
be 
noted 
that, 
unlike 
requirements for plumbing fixtures, this section 
does not establish that a certain number of 
passenger elevators are required to be 
accessible while other passenger elevators 
may be of a design that is not fully accessible. 
All passenger elevators that are on an 
accessible route are required to be accessible. 
If a bank of passenger elevators is provided as 
part of an accessible route, all elevators in that 
bank must be accessible. See SBC 901 
Section 306.7.7 for existing elevators being 
altered. 
-
pages.code_text:
CHAPTER 11—ACCESSIBILITY 
 
 
SBC 201-CC-2024 
1344 
 
both high and low drinking fountains to be 
designed for children. There are technical 
criteria in ICC A117.1 for wheelchair accessible 
drinking fountains specifically designed for 
children who are age 12 and younger. This may 
be appropriate for day care facilities and 
certain areas of elementary schools. 
1110.6  1110.6 Bottle-filling stations.  Where 
bottle-filling stations are provided, they shall be 
accessible. 
Exception: Bottle-filling stations over drinking 
fountains for standing persons are not required to be 
accessible, provided that bottle-filling stations are 
also located over the drinking fountains for persons 
using wheelchairs.
pages.commentary:
In the interest of reducing the use of disposable 
bottles for water, many schools are providing 
bottle-filling 
stations. 
Due 
to 
security 
limitations, bottle-filling stations are also being 
provided in airports. All operable parts intended 
for use by occupants must be accessible. 
 
The exception is in recognition that the 
requirements for standing drinking fountains 
potentially will not allow for compliant reach 
over an obstruction. When the bottle-filling 
stations are provided over the wheelchair 
drinking fountains, having them also over the 
standing drinking fountains would be providing 
redundant facilities. 
1110.7  Saunas 
and 
steam 
rooms. 
Where 
provided, saunas and steam rooms shall be 
accessible. 
Exception: Where saunas or steam rooms are 
clustered at a single location, at least 5 percent of 
the saunas and steam rooms, but not less than one, 
of each type in each cluster shall be accessible.

❖ Since 2009, ICC A117.1 has included technical 
requirements for saunas and steam rooms. 
The scoping in SBC 201 is consistent with 
bathrooms and coordinates with the 2010 ADA 
Standard. What constitutes a “cluster” is 
subjective; however, typically the rooms would 
have to have entrances immediately adjacent 
to or across the hall from one another to be 
considered “clustered.” 
 
The requirements in the standard provide for 
entering the room, closing the door and then 
transferring to the bench in the room. Removing 
the wheelchair from the space is not addressed, 
so perhaps a special chair might be utilized. 
Section 914 of SBC 501 addresses safety 
requirements for sauna rooms. 
1110.8  Elevators. Passenger elevators on an 
accessible route shall be accessible and comply 
with Chapter 30.

❖ This section requires all passenger elevators 
on an accessible route to be accessible. The 
reference to Chapter 30 requires all passenger

standards as well as accessibility standards.

with a car, that moves within guides and serves 
two or more landings … “ The limitations of use 
for the different types of elevators are 
controlled by the standard.

“construction

accessible route. Therefore, freight and

pages.text:
CHAPTER 11—ACCESSIBILITY 
 
 
SBC 201-CC-2024 
1345 
 
 
The reference to an accessible route (see 
Sections 1104.3, 1104.4 and 1110.8) is 
important because some areas in certain 
buildings may not be required to be accessible 
(see Sections 1103.2 and 1104.4). For 
example, since an elevator penthouse is not 
required to be accessible (see Section 
1103.2.9), the passenger elevator is not 
required to serve that level. Keep in mind that 
the 
penthouse 
that 
contains 
elevator 
equipment must be accessed by a stairway 
(see Section 1011.12.1). In addition, certain 
types of elevators (e.g., freight elevators) may 
not be part of an accessible route. 
 
Note that there are additional requirements for 
elevators depending on the building height: 
• 
In buildings four stories and higher, at least 
one elevator must be sized in order to 
accommodate 
stretchers 
(see 
Section 
3002.4 ). 
• 
In buildings five stories and higher, an 
elevator must be available for serving as part 
of an accessible means of egress (see 
Section 1009.2.1 ). 
• 
Buildings 36 m or taller shall have two fire 
service access elevators, both sized to fit a 
stretcher (see Sections 403 and 3007). 
• 
Occupant evacuation elevators are also an 
option (see Section 3008). 
❖ 
Note that fire service access elevators and 
occupant evacuation elevators can also serve 
as elevators that are part of an accessible 
means of egress. Two-way communication 
must be provided in elevator lobbies in 
sprinklered buildings (see Section 1009.8 ). 
1110.9  Lifts. Platform (wheelchair) lifts are 
permitted to be a part of a required accessible route 
in new construction where indicated in Items 1 
through 10. Platform (wheelchair) lifts shall be 
installed in accordance with ASME A18.1. 
1 
An accessible route to a performing area and 
speaker platforms. 
2 
An accessible route to wheelchair spaces 
required to comply with the wheelchair 
space dispersion requirements of Sections 
1109.2.2 through 1109.2.6 . 
3 
An accessible route to spaces that are not 
open to the general public with an occupant 
load of not more than five. 
4 
An accessible route within an individual 
dwelling unit or sleeping unit required to be 
an Accessible unit, Type A unit or Type B 
unit. 
5 
An accessible route to jury boxes and witness 
stands; raised courtroom stations including 
judges’ benches, clerks’ stations, bailiffs’ 
stations, deputy clerks’ stations and court 
reporters’ stations; and to depressed areas 
such as the well of the court. 
6 
An accessible route to load and unload areas 
serving amusement rides. 
7 
An accessible route to play components or 
soft contained play structures. 
8 
An accessible route to team or player seating 
areas serving areas of sport activity. 
9 
An accessible route instead of gangways 
serving recreational boating facilities and 
fishing piers and platforms. 
10 An accessible route where existing exterior 
site constraints make use of a ramp or 
elevator infeasible. 
❖ 
This 
section 
indicates 
that 
platform 
(wheelchair) lifts are only permitted to be part 
of a required accessible route in new 
construction in limited situations. If a platform 
lift is permitted as part of the accessible route 
into a space, it can also serve as part of the 
accessible route used for means of egress out 
of the space (with the exception of Item 10) if it 
has standby power (see Section 1007.5). 
There are concerns about the potential for a 
large number of people needing to exit the 
building under the allowances for Item 10; 
therefore, an accessible means of egress must 
be via another route. Platform lifts may be used 
more extensively in existing construction (see 
SBC 901 Section 306.7.8). This is in 
recognition that circumstances in existing 
buildings 
may 
make 
it 
impractical 
to 
accomplish accessibility by use of an elevator, 
in which case a platform lift is a reasonable 
alternative. Accessible means of egress is not 
required in existing buildings undergoing 
alterations (see SBC 901 306.7.2); therefore, a 
platform lift as part of the accessible means of 
egress in these situations is not an issue.  
 
A previous edition of the technical standard 
(i.e., 
ASME 
A17.1-1996) 
required 
key 
operation to platform lifts, which unnecessarily 
inhibited independent access by persons with 
physical disabilities. The requirements for 
platform lifts have been removed from the 
elevator standard and now have their own 
standard, ASME A18.1, Safety Standard for 
Platform Lifts and Stairway Chairlifts. This 
newer standard allows push-button operation 
of platform lifts, thus making independent 
access much easier. 
-

CHAPTER 11—ACCESSIBILITY 
 
 
SBC 201-CC-2024 
1345 
 
 
The reference to an accessible route (see 
Sections 1104.3, 1104.4 and 1110.8) is 
important because some areas in certain

section

that

(i.e., 
ASME 
A17.1-1996) 
required 
key 
operation to platform lifts, which unnecessarily 
inhibited independent access by persons with

pages.text:
CHAPTER 11—ACCESSIBILITY 
 
 
SBC 201-CC-2024 
1346 
 
 
The listed items indicate where a platform lift 
can be utilized as part of a required accessible 
route in new construction. Items 1 and 2 allow 
platform lifts to be utilized for access to 
performing (e.g., stages, orchestra pits) and 
wheelchair space locations used to view an 
event. 
 
Item 3 specifies that a platform lift may be used 
to provide access to a non-public area with five 
or less occupants, such as a projection booth. 
 
Item 4 allows for a platform lift within an 
individual dwelling or sleeping unit. 
 
Item 5 permits platform lifts to provide access 
to the raised or lowered areas typically found in 
courtroom settings. This is consistent with 
Section 1109.4.1.4 . 
 
Item 6 allows platform lifts for access to loading 
and unloading areas serving amusement rides. 
It does not attempt to address access to any 
other element of a ride. There are provisions 
for amusement rides in Section 1111.4.8 and 
Chapter 10 of the 2017 ICC A117.1. 
 
Item 7 identifies children’s play spaces, the 
entry to which may be raised or depressed, as 
an appropriate place for a platform lift. Such 
spaces are often associated with restaurants, 
shopping malls and pre-schools. There are 
provisions for children’s play areas in Chapter 
10 of the 2017 ICC A117.1. 
 
Item 8 acknowledges that team or player 
seating may be a small, depressed area (i.e., 
dugout) capable of being efficiently served by 
a platform lift. 
 
Item 9 allows for a platform lift to serve as part 
of the accessible route to recreational boating 
and fishing piers required to be on an 
accessible route per Sections 1111.4.9 and 
1111.4.11 . 
 
Item 
10 
recognizes 
that 
existing 
site 
constraints may make installation of a ramp or 
elevator infeasible. An example would be the 
situation of dealing with existing public 
sidewalks, easements and public ways in 
downtown urban areas. This situation would be 
most common in hilly areas where the street 
and sidewalk follows grade and the building’s 
floor is level, resulting in steps up or down at 
entrances. It should be noted that in this 
situation, while a platform lift can be used for 
the accessible route into a space, it cannot be 
used as one of the required accessible means 
of egress (see Section 1009.5 ) 
 
Note that a platform lift that was not part of the 
required accessible route could be used to 
facilitate access to any space. The governing 
factor in those situations would be the 
limitations of the product itself for capacity and 
travel distance. Item 4 permits platform lifts to 
be utilized within individual dwelling or sleeping 
units as part of a required accessible route. 
Single-family homes are not required to be 
accessible. However, persons with mobility 
impairments may choose to install stairway 
chairlifts in their private homes. Section 1011.2 
, Exception 3, specifies a minimum stairway 
width for platform lifts or chair lifts provided 
within dwelling units. 
 
A platform lift is an electrically operated, 
mechanical device designed to transport a 
person who cannot use stairs over a short 
vertical distance. Platform lifts must be sized to 
accommodate a wheelchair. Platform lifts can 
be used by persons who use wheelchairs or 
scooters and persons with limited mobility. 
Some platform lifts are equipped with folding 
seats. A fold-down seat that moves up the 
stairway is not a platform lift, but rather a chair 
lift. A stairway chair lift is not suitable for a 
person using a wheelchair and cannot serve as 
part of a required accessible route. A platform 
lift is most suitable for changes of elevation of 
one story or less where the installation of a 
ramp is not feasible. ASME A18.1 limits the rise 
of platform lifts to 4200 mm or less. [see 
Commentary Figure 1110.9(2)]. 
 
While new technologies are expanding current 
options, there are two basic types of platform 
lifts:  vertical lifts and inclined lifts. Vertical lifts 
are similar to elevators in that they travel only 
up and down in a fixed vertical space. Platform 
lifts should not be confused with Limited 
Access Limited Use elevators or private 
residence elevators (see Section 1110.8 ). 
Inclined platform lifts are usually installed in 
conjunction with a stairway and travel along the 
slope of the stairway. Inclined lifts are a design 
consideration for long flights of stairs where a 
vertical platform lift is not practical, where 
headroom is limited or where ceilings are low. 
1110.10  
Storage. Where fixed or built-in 
storage elements such as cabinets, coat hooks, 
shelves, medicine cabinets, lockers, closets and 
drawers are provided in required accessible spaces, 
at least 5 percent, but not less than one of each type 
shall be accessible. 
❖ 
Fixed or built-in storage elements are common 
in many occupancies, including both residential 
and non-residential occupancies such as office 
buildings, recreational facilities, etc. Examples 
would include coat closets, storage closets, 
lockers, etc. The code does not require storage 
facilities, but when such facilities are provided, 
this section requires at least 5 percent, but not 
-

shall be accessible.

in many occupancies, including both residential 
and non-residential occupancies such as office

pages.text:
CHAPTER 11—ACCESSIBILITY 
 
 
SBC 201-CC-2024 
1347 
 
fewer than one, of each type to contain storage 
space in accordance with ICC A117.1. This 
could be a portion of shelving or coat rods, a 
number of lockers, etc. Requirements would 
include a wheelchair space in front of the 
element, operable parts (if applicable) and the 
shelf, box or hook within the 375 to 1200 mm 
reach ranges. Where lockers are located in 
locker rooms where people are expected to 
change clothes, see Section 1110.14. 
 
Kitchens and kitchenettes required to be 
accessible are more specifically addressed in 
Section 804 of ICC A117.1. For the kitchen or 
kitchenette to be able to have both upper and 
lower cabinets at heights that keep the counter 
useable, storage elements are not required to 
meet the reach range. Other storage elements 
within Accessible and Type A units are 
specifically addressed under Chapter 11 of ICC 
A117.1. Type B units do not have requirements 
for storage elements. 
 
Storage elements that would be part of an 
employee workspace, such as a lab in a 
doctor's office, are exempted under Section 
1103.2.2. 
1110.10.1  
Equity. Accessible facilities and 
spaces shall be provided with the same storage 
elements as provided in the similar non-accessible 
facilities and spaces. 
❖ 
This section is really about the issue of equal 
access to amenities. This requirement is 
consistent with the requirements for family or 
assisted-use bathrooms to have the same 
lockers, hand dryers, baby-changing stations, 
etc., as provided in the men's and women's 
bathrooms. 
For 
example, 
hotel 
rooms 
commonly provide closets and dressers for 
guests. These same storage facilities must 
also be available in Accessible sleeping units. 
They cannot be eliminated as an option to 
providing an accessible route or turning space 
within the Accessible unit. For hotel rooms, ICC 
A117.1 has a requirement for providing equal 
bathroom counter space in similar Accessible 
and nonaccessible guestrooms. 
1110.10.2  
Shelving and display units. Self-
service shelves and display units shall be located on 
an accessible route. Such shelving and display units 
shall not be required to comply with reach-range 
provisions. 
❖ 
This section is intended to provide sightlines to 
items on shelves for persons who utilize 
wheelchairs. Someone may be required to 
assist them in reaching the items, as putting all 
items within the 375 to 1200 mm reach range 
would adversely affect the space allowed for 
products or information. 
1110.11  
Detectable warnings. Passenger 
transit platform edges bordering a drop-off and not 
protected by platform screens or guards shall have 
a detectable warning. 
Exception: Detectable warnings are not required at 
bus stops. 
❖ 
A detectable warning is a standardized feature 
built in or applied to walking surfaces to warn a 
visually impaired person of a hazard on or near 
his or her path of travel and which could result 
in injury to that person, such as a street or 
significant drop-off. 
 
This section requires a detectable warning at 
the edges of passenger transit platforms where 
they border a drop-off (see Commentary Figure 
1110.11). For example, loading platforms in 
both light- and heavy-rail transit stations where 
passengers await the arrival of trains are 
serious potential hazards to a person with a 
vision impairment when there is no train at the 
station. The presence of a detectable warning 
at the platform edge where it borders the drop-
off would be encountered and recognized by a 
person with a vision impairment either through 
detection by a long cane or by foot contact. 
This is also a good safety feature for all 
passengers. 
 
The exception for bus stops is established so 
as not to require a detectable warning at the 
curb between a sidewalk and the street. 
Detectable warnings at curbs are contradictory 
to the envisioned application of detectable 
warnings in the built environment. The curb 
itself is a recognizable and well-known cue to 
people with vision impairments to proceed with 
caution. 
 
There is a great deal of controversy and 
general disagreement as to the benefit or 
advisability of detectable warning surfaces 
throughout the built environment. The use of 
such warnings at transit stations is, to date, the 
only well-documented use of detectable 
warnings, as discussed in studies such as 
“Tactile Warnings to Promote Safety in the 
Vicinity of Transit Platform Edges,” conducted 
by 
the 
Urban 
Mass 
Transportation 
Administration, and “Pathfinder Tactile Tile 
Demonstration Test Project,” conducted by the 
Metro-Dade Transit Agency. The Access 
Board looked into this issue as part of the 
development of the Public Rights-of-Way 
Guidelines for intersections of public sidewalks 
and roads. The 2010 ADA Standard dropped 
the requirement for detectable warnings at curb 
-
pages.code_text:
CHAPTER 11—ACCESSIBILITY 
 
 
SBC 201-CC-2024 
1347 
 
fewer than one, of each type to contain storage 
space in accordance with ICC A117.1. This 
could be a portion of shelving or coat rods, a 
number of lockers, etc. Requirements would 
include a wheelchair space in front of the 
element, operable parts (if applicable) and the 
shelf, box or hook within the 375 to 1200 mm 
reach ranges. Where lockers are located in 
locker rooms where people are expected to 
change clothes, see Section 1110.14. 
 
Kitchens and kitchenettes required to be 
accessible are more specifically addressed in 
Section 804 of ICC A117.1. For the kitchen or 
kitchenette to be able to have both upper and 
lower cabinets at heights that keep the counter 
useable, storage elements are not required to 
meet the reach range. Other storage elements 
within Accessible and Type A units are 
specifically addressed under Chapter 11 of ICC 
A117.1. Type B units do not have requirements 
for storage elements. 
 
Storage elements that would be part of an 
employee workspace, such as a lab in a 
doctor's office, are exempted under Section 
1103.2.2. 
1110.10.1  
Equity. Accessible facilities and 
spaces shall be provided with the same storage 
elements as provided in the similar non-accessible 
facilities and spaces.
pages.commentary:
This section is really about the issue of equal 
access to amenities. This requirement is 
consistent with the requirements for family or 
assisted-use bathrooms to have the same 
lockers, hand dryers, baby-changing stations, 
etc., as provided in the men's and women's 
bathrooms. 
For 
example, 
hotel 
rooms 
commonly provide closets and dressers for 
guests. These same storage facilities must 
also be available in Accessible sleeping units. 
They cannot be eliminated as an option to 
providing an accessible route or turning space 
within the Accessible unit. For hotel rooms, ICC 
A117.1 has a requirement for providing equal 
bathroom counter space in similar Accessible 
and nonaccessible guestrooms. 
1110.10.2  
Shelving and display units. Self-
service shelves and display units shall be located on 
an accessible route. Such shelving and display units 
shall not be required to comply with reach-range 
provisions.

❖ This section is intended to provide sightlines to 
items on shelves for persons who utilize 
wheelchairs. Someone may be required to 
assist them in reaching the items, as putting all 
items within the 375 to 1200 mm reach range 
would adversely affect the space allowed for 
products or information. 
1110.11  
Detectable warnings. Passenger 
transit platform edges bordering a drop-off and not 
protected by platform screens or guards shall have 
a detectable warning. 
Exception: Detectable warnings are not required at 
bus stops.

❖ A detectable warning is a standardized feature 
built in or applied to walking surfaces to warn a 
visually impaired person of a hazard on or near 
his or her path of travel and which could result 
in injury to that person, such as a street or 
significant drop-off. 
 
This section requires a detectable warning at 
the edges of passenger transit platforms where 
they border a drop-off (see Commentary Figure 
1110.11). For example, loading platforms in 
both light- and heavy-rail transit stations where 
passengers await the arrival of trains are 
serious potential hazards to a person with a 
vision impairment when there is no train at the 
station. The presence of a detectable warning 
at the platform edge where it borders the drop-
off would be encountered and recognized by a 
person with a vision impairment either through 
detection by a long cane or by foot contact. 
This is also a good safety feature for all 
passengers. 
 
The exception for bus stops is established so 
as not to require a detectable warning at the 
curb between a sidewalk and the street. 
Detectable warnings at curbs are contradictory 
to the envisioned application of detectable 
warnings in the built environment. The curb 
itself is a recognizable and well-known cue to 
people with vision impairments to proceed with 
caution. 
 
There is a great deal of controversy and 
general disagreement as to the benefit or 
advisability of detectable warning surfaces 
throughout the built environment. The use of 
such warnings at transit stations is, to date, the 
only well-documented use of detectable 
warnings, as discussed in studies such as 
“Tactile Warnings to Promote Safety in the 
Vicinity of Transit Platform Edges,” conducted 
by 
the 
Urban 
Mass 
Transportation 
Administration, and “Pathfinder Tactile Tile 
Demonstration Test Project,” conducted by the 
Metro-Dade Transit Agency. The Access 
Board looked into this issue as part of the 
development of the Public Rights-of-Way 
Guidelines for intersections of public sidewalks 
and roads. The 2010 ADA Standard dropped 
the requirement for detectable warnings at curb 
-

pages.text:
CHAPTER 11—ACCESSIBILITY 
 
 
SBC 201-CC-2024 
1348 
 
cuts. Neither the code nor ICC A117.1 requires 
detectable warnings at curb cuts. ICC A117.1 
only addresses the technical criteria for when 
someone chooses to provide detectable 
warnings. 
 
ICC A117.1 prescribes the type of surface that 
constitutes a detectable warning in Section 
705. One option for the detectable warning 
surface that is currently considered suitable 
consists of raised, truncated domes with a 
diameter between 23 mm and 36 mm, a height 
of approximately 5.1 mm and center-to-center 
spacing of between 40 mm and 60 mm. 
However, there is current and ongoing 
research into the use and application of 
truncated domes as a suitable detectable 
warning surface for various applications, 
including interior and exterior locations. ICC 
A117.1 requires that the type of detectable 
warning surface utilized must be standard 
throughout a building, facility, site or complex 
of buildings. If different types of detectable 
warning surfaces are utilized, their usefulness 
is diminished. The different messages they 
would convey to a person with a vision 
impairment would not be consistent and may 
be confusing and easily misinterpreted. 
 
It is anticipated that future applications of 
detectable warnings will be considered when 
additional research and documentation of their 
usefulness and suitability is available. One 
issue, among many, is the durability of a 
detectable warning surface in an exterior 
application and the potential difficulty that 
truncated domes may present to a person in a 
wheelchair, as well as to non-disabled persons 
who may have to negotiate the surface. 
1110.12  
Seating at tables, counters and 
work surfaces. Where seating or standing space at 
fixed or built-in tables, counters or work surfaces is 
provided in accessible spaces, at least 5 percent of 
the seating and standing spaces, but not less than 
one, shall be accessible. 
Exception: Check-writing surfaces at check-out 
aisles not required to comply with Section 
1110.13.1 are not required to be accessible. 
❖ 
Many 
occupancies, 
such 
as 
libraries, 
classrooms and other public spaces, are 
equipped with fixed seating, tables or both (see 
Commentary Figure 1110.12). The threshold of 
5 percent is consistent with the requirement for 
accessible seating in dining areas as specified 
in Section 1109.2.9.1 . Fixed or built-in seating 
that is part of an individual workstation is not 
required to be accessible (see Section 
1103.2.2 ). Where required, accessible tables 
and work surfaces are expected to include 
adequate knee and toe clearances as specified 
in ICC A117.1. For requirements for points of 
sales or service that do not include a 
workstation, see Section 1110.13.2 . For 
example, the check-writing station in the center 
of the bank lobby is considered a workstation, 
whereas 
the 
teller 
window 
is 
typically 
considered a service counter. The accessible 
check-writing station would require knee and 
toe clearances, while the accessible teller 
window would not. 
 
Note that the requirement addresses built-in 
counters, tables or workstations for standing or 
seating. Built-in surfaces where persons are 
typically standing are most often installed at a 
height of 900 to 1200 mm. Depending on the 
overall configuration of the counters in the 
space, a portion of the counter for standing 
persons may need to be lowered to an 
accessible height of 700 to 850 mm and 
configured for forward approach. 
 
The exception clarifies that a check-writing 
surface provided at a nonaccessible checkout 
aisle is not required to meet the counter and 
work surface requirements in ICC A117.1 (see 
Section 1110.13.1). 
 
There are allowances in ICC A117.1 for work 
surfaces specifically designed for children's 
use. This may be appropriate in facilities such 
as day care facilities and certain areas of 
elementary schools, or areas frequented 
mostly 
by children, 
such 
as 
children's 
museums or the children's areas in libraries. 
1110.12.1  
Dispersion. Accessible fixed or 
built-in seating at tables, counters or work surfaces 
shall be distributed throughout the space or facility 
containing such elements and located on a level 
accessed by an accessible route. 
❖ 
An appropriate distribution of seating and 
tables is required and is intended to be 
consistent with the distribution of all such 
seating and tables in the facility. For example, 
a library may contain several separate areas in 
which fixed seating or tables are installed. This 
provision would prohibit the location of all 
accessible seating and tables in a single 
specific area of the facility. However, this 
should not be construed to mean that every 
separate area in which fixed seating or tables 
are provided is required to contain an 
accessible seat or table. For example, if there 
are four areas in which fixed seating or tables 
are located, and two such seats or tables are 
required by this section to be accessible, this 
section requires that one accessible seat or 
table be located in two of the four areas. 
-

CHAPTER 11—ACCESSIBILITY 
 
 
SBC 201-CC-2024 
1348 
 
cuts. Neither the code nor ICC A117.1 requires 
detectable warnings at curb cuts. ICC A117.1 
only addresses the technical criteria for when 
someone chooses to provide detectable 
warnings.

705. One option for the detectable warning

Seating at tables, counters and 
work surfaces. Where seating or standing space at

provided in accessible spaces, at least 5 percent of 
the seating and standing spaces, but not less than 
one, shall be accessible. 
Exception: Check-writing surfaces at check-out 
aisles not required to comply with Section 
1110.13.1 are not required to be accessible.

Dispersion. Accessible fixed or 
built-in seating at tables, counters or work surfaces 
shall be distributed throughout the space or facility 
containing such elements and located on a level 
accessed by an accessible route.

❖ An appropriate distribution of seating and 
tables is required and is intended to be 
consistent with the distribution of all such

are provided is required to contain an

are located, and two such seats or tables are
